articles.title: 4K years ago, Mohenjo-daro grew more equal over time
articles.summary: Researchers from the University of York have found that Mohenjo-daro, an ancient city in present-day Pakistan, grew more equal over time by examining housing patterns and economic inequality levels. The study suggests that as the city developed, inequality dropped instead of rising, with house sizes becoming more similar during later periods of growth. This finding challenges the long-held assumption that cities grow alongside inequality, and highlights the importance of practical systems in limiting social disparities.

articles.title: Now AI agents need what RSS does
articles.summary: RSS provides a predictable and structured way for AI agents to consume new content, including podcast episodes, written articles, and newsletters, by offering a deterministic list of what is new without rate limits or authentication walls. This is in contrast to social platform APIs that often revoke access on a quarterly basis and charge for it. As a result, RSS is well-suited for applications such as language model retrieval, monitoring agent checks, and summarisation tools, making it an essential protocol for developers working with AI agents.

articles.title: My thoughts after using Clojure for about a month
articles.summary: Clojure's ergonomic design has won me over after playing around with it for about a month. Its unique blend of programming paradigms and name schemes makes it more cohesive than Common Lisp, which was written in the early 80s as a compromise among various lisps. Clojure's seq abstraction simplifies mapping and makes equality checks easier, allowing developers to focus on their code rather than learning new syntax. While there are caveats, I haven't encountered them yet, making it an attractive choice for developers looking to learn Clojure.

articles.title: Open Repair Data Standard – Open Repair Alliance
articles.summary: The Open Repair Data Standard (ORDS) aims to provide a shared approach to collecting and sharing repair data on small electricals and electronics, enabling global identification of trends and patterns by combining data from local community events. The standard defines a common format for repairing data about these products, focusing on three main areas: product-related information, repair-related details, and session-related data. By providing an open Creative Commons license, the ORDS allows for easy aggregation and comparison of repair data, making it easier to identify recurrent failures and fixes in various communities worldwide.

articles.title: Self-calling executables
articles.summary: Self-calling executables allow developers to run another version of an executable directly from within the current one, providing isolation and flexibility in testing scenarios or command-line tools. This technique can be useful when dealing with global state issues in Go programs. By setting specific environment variables, such as TEST_MAIN, developers can control how tests are executed, enabling more efficient use of resources and reducing interference between multiple tests running simultaneously.

articles.title: iddqd, or the hardest kind of unsafe Rust
articles.summary: iddqd, or the hardest kind of unsafe Rust, is a library for maps that uses borrowed values from keys to store records. This approach can lead to unpredictable behavior if not managed carefully, as it requires correct handling of in-memory indexes and duplicate storage of emails across key and value. The library's use of safe code underneath has raised concerns about the amount of unsafe code in Rust rewrites, prompting the author to examine iddqd's implementation and discuss ways to tame its unsafe aspects.

articles.title: How we index images for RAG
articles.summary: We've developed an indexing system for images in our RAG pipeline that allows us to describe each image once at indexing time with a cheap vision model, store the descriptions as text, and retrieve them alongside ordinary text chunks. This approach has proven effective in reducing per-query overhead by 1% to 6%. By using images to augment text-only answers, we've seen statistically significant improvements in user satisfaction, particularly for complex engineering questions. Our system is designed to be efficient and cost-effective, making it suitable for large-scale use cases without the need for expensive vision models.

The removal of the X11 session in Plasma 6.8 will be accompanied by a mass cleanup of X11-specific code, with all affected applications losing their X11 support. This change ensures compatibility and stability for users on Wayland-only systems, while also allowing for performance improvements and new features to be implemented through the use of Wayland.

articles.title: Memory safety is a matter of life and death
articles.summary: Memory safety is a matter of life and death. The work we do affects real people, often unconsciously, and buffer overflows can be devastating. A security team's access to data and water cooler talk about agentic bug-finding models has led to the "vulnpocalypse," a term coined by the security industry to describe the catastrophic impact of memory-unsafe software on high-quality bug-finding agents. The medium-term impact is clear: open-source software written in memory-unsafe languages will be vulnerable to exploits, with some actors using them for cyber crimes and others potentially killing people. This makes switching to memory safe languages a moral imperative, especially when alternatives like Carbon exist but are not yet widely available.
